qa_pairs.question: How can a user terminate all `vncserver` processes running under their username on a node instantly?
qa_pairs.answer: To instantly kill all `vncserver` processes running under your username on a node, you can run the command `pkill Xvnc -u $USER`.

qa_pairs.question: What command is used to display all `vncserver` sessions running under a user's account?
qa_pairs.answer: All `vncserver` sessions running under your username (on a login or compute node) can be displayed using the `vncserver -list` command.

qa_pairs.question: What is the purpose of the `-AlwaysShared` option when starting a `vncserver`?
qa_pairs.answer: If a `vncserver` is started with the additional `-AlwaysShared` option, it allows multiple connections to be made to that specific VNC server from any remote location.

qa_pairs.question: What is the advantage of starting a `vncserver` with the `-AlwaysShared` option for users who need to connect from different locations?
qa_pairs.answer: Starting a `vncserver` with the `-AlwaysShared` option enables users to establish new tunnels and `vncviewer` connections from any remote location (e.g., from the office and then from home) to access the same desktop and seamlessly continue working without needing to shut down and restart the desktop.

qa_pairs.question: What happens if a `vncserver` is not started with the `-AlwaysShared` option and a user attempts to make multiple connections or reconnect from a new location?
qa_pairs.answer: If your `vncserver` was not started with `-AlwaysShared`, only one `vncviewer` connection will be possible. To reconnect from a new location, you would need to close all applications running in the desktop, shut down your `vncserver`, and then restart a whole new desktop and all applications from scratch.
